item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My criticism is the same as many other people here: the initial stuff was chilling and perplexing, and I lost interest in the logs of "an ancient ruler is amazed by modern technology."

The writing's good, and I appreciate that you didn't make him an unnecessary idiot, but the concept is just too widespread for it to stand on its own for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very enjoyable to read. The SCP itself feels almost like a detective story, where the the events are a product of both the perpetrator’s actions and mistakes along the way. If that makes any sense.

Team Leader: Looks like Shaporov was right,

Pretty sure that name is misspelled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realized it was going to be about opening Qin Shi Huangdi pretty early, which I thought was really cool. The mystery also seemed to be progressing nicely, and I was curious to see where it went. But then, the disappearance plot was dropped to focus on overly expository narration from the emperor. The guy who's famous for burning books deciding to keep immaculate notes in a world where he knows no one can read what he's saying breaks my immersion, and the non-end of the main mystery of why this disaster occurred when his tomb was unearthed leaves me wanting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Marvelous entry. Please add more addendum to this ancient king who is lost in our world. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this was a fun read and a nifty concept. the ending could have stood to be stronger, but I was not bored at any point and really enjoyed reading this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was so great. I love anomalies that only affect the Foundation, and the mystery of "just what the fuck happened?" was so cool to experience.

Then we get this ancient Chinese emperor showing up again, and I love how he catalogs everything he finds in our world as we might have cataloged his. That, I think, is the best part of this piece.

The desire to live forever is a curse if the world you come back to is not yours anymore.

This stuck with me. I love how, despite the fact he's shown to be a ruthless killer, I ended up feeling sorry for him by the end. He was just… lost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love historical SCPs, and the "mystery" theme of the contest is present, but I was hoping for something a bit less overdone than the good old "historical character who wanted to be immortal is in fact immortal", especially with that particular emperor. Also, might just be personal taste but I tend to dislike it when characters from the past think nearly every piece of modern technology is clearly "magic". Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I made no mistake, I am sure what you try to imply is the ruler of Qin. If so，I have to say there are several historical mistakes about the notes he left, for there' re something he could never know as a slumpering king.

First：Chinese in Qin Dynasty have no paper to use, but the parchment is unusual either, or even maybe not existed as a way of recording. They just use bumboos or wooden blocks connected by ropes to write and record, something simliar to scrolls.

Second: Ancient Chinese know nothing about glass, what similar to it may be cystial or gems, there are actrually an cystial artifict, which is a transparent cup made of clean cystial be found in a ancient tomb older than Qin Dynasty. Either way, no glass exist at that time.

Third: I don't know if you're intented, but as a ruler of ancient Chinese dynasty, one only calls themselves as "孤"(longly)、“朕”(not-perfect)、“寡人”（longly one）and so on. These tiltes are literally negtive and strange, for they have to show their "Modesty"; Aslo a styled reprenstion for holding the Apex-power. It's some thing similar to "Royal we", and they will never just call themselves as "I" or "Me", as long as they still wanna to rule the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, just wow. I loved how you turned the mystery aspect into not something that the foundation must unravel, but something that the scip itself is facing. Nothing more to add that others haven't just that the quality here is gold. +1

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

There are some parts of this that I really like, and others that I don't enjoy as much. The majority of this was excellent; you did something really interesting with this anomaly and definitely implemented the mystery theme pretty well. All that I've really got for these sections is a couple of minor nitpicks.

Myung: Wainwright is correct. I've seen skeletons from ancient sites in Turkey. These bones don't look about the same age.

I'm pretty sure that there shouldn't be that 'don't' in there.

(a Foundation AI that automatically translates one language into another for easier communication)

I feel this could be worded better. Maybe something like 'A Foundation AI-powered translation software' or anything similarly brief.

When anyone reads a piece of work, they tend to remember the beginning and end of it more than anything else. It sticks in their mind more, and it's always important that both are good. You definitely start strong, as already mentioned. However, I don't feel as confident about the ending. The majority of the final addendum was fairly uninteresting; I've seen things in fiction where ancient people react identically to technology before, and this did nothing new in that aspect. The interleaved regret over immortality redeems it slightly, but it's too little characterisation to fully cover up the issues I have. The very end also improved to some extent, though it links too strongly to the fairly generic aspects of waking up in the relative future.

Overall, I feel like this was generally pretty good, but was dragged back by the final section being something I personally didn't care for. It's a novote, but leaning more towards positive than negative. While I completely get it if you think this is finished as a piece, some more exploration into the king as a character beyond what there is currently could work wonders.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just love skips based in history so of course this one gets a +1.
I just wonder how it interracts with 2101? I know there's no canon and all, but still it could be interesting to link the two!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Terracotta, 37 years of rule and the elixir of life, definitely him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is, is the entity that Ying Zheng?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i was very much enjoying this piece up until the time of the logs, at which point i felt like everything lost steam. that being said, i enjoy the first part of this too much to downvote, and i really like the premise of the foundation dealing with an ancient chinese king. i just really wish that would have been explored more instead of how it turned out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. It doesn't present one mystery, but kind of 2 mysteries. And while they're not the most complicated mysteries in the world, their parallels and interplay are really entertaining and interesting to see foiled throughout the various logs.

Hard +1.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
We in the present have an obsession with peering back into the past and trying to solve its mysteries. If the past was given the chance to peer back… how would it react?

This is my 5k. Credit to the following for crit - Henzoid, Yossipossi, JackalRelated, Modulum, Elogee FishTruck, Popsioak, WinterShadow, Tanhony, Uncle Nicolini, Cerastes, Almarduk, Captain Kirby
